skill.name: framework-migration-deps-upgrade
skill.description: Plans safe dependency and framework upgrades with risk assessment, migration steps, testing guidance, rollback planning, and post-upgrade validation.
skill.summary: Plan dependency and framework upgrades with controlled risk and clear validation steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command, network, and weak-cryptography patterns. Manual review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or exfiltration; most weak-cryptography and Markdown backtick findings are false positives from prose and code fences. The real risk is that examples encourage package-manager, npx, rollback, and health-check commands that can modify files or run third-party code.

medium_findings.title: Package Manager Commands Can Execute Third-Party Code
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes npm, npx, pip, and framework upgrade commands. This is expected for dependency migration, but package managers and codemods can run project scripts or downloaded code, so execution needs user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly present and can execute package-manager workflows. The context is legitimate dependency migration, so this is a controlled-use risk rather than evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Rollback Examples Modify and Delete Workspace Files
medium_findings.description: The rollback script copies package files, moves backup files, removes node_modules, and deletes an upgrade branch. These operations are useful but can lose local state if run without checking the workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell operations are explicit in the playbook and affect files and git branches. They are not malicious, but the operational impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Localhost Health Check Network Call
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL found is a curl check against localhost. It does not show evidence of data exfiltration or contact with an external service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly localhost and appears in rollback verification. No external endpoint or sensitive data transfer was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at prose, headings, and output-format lines. Manual review did not find cryptographic APIs, hashing logic, or insecure encryption guidance in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged locations and found descriptive dependency-upgrade text, not crypto code. This strongly indicates scanner keyword false positives.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown fences or inline resource paths. They are not executable Ruby backtick calls in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is Markdown formatting around examples, not Ruby execution. Some fenced examples still contain real shell guidance, which is tracked separately as medium risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Package Inspection Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook shows Python subprocess.run calls for npm outdated and pip outdated. Arguments are static arrays, reducing injection risk, but the examples still execute local package-manager commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are visible and use fixed argument arrays. Risk exists through command execution, not through string-based command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Rollback Script Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The rollback example uses shell commands to back up files, restore package files, remove dependencies, reinstall packages, run tests, and call a local health endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell block is explicit and operationally powerful. It is clearly framed as rollback guidance, so the risk is misuse rather than hidden intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Commands Include Unsanitized Placeholders
dangerous_patterns.description: Several generated bash examples interpolate package names and versions into npm and git commands. If copied with untrusted values, this could produce unsafe commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholders are visible inside shell command examples. The file does not implement execution, so exploitation depends on downstream use by an agent or user.

content.value_statement: Dependency and framework upgrades can break builds, tests, and runtime behavior. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan staged upgrades with validation and rollback steps.
actual_capabilities: Assess outdated npm and Python dependencies from package-manager output.
Classify updates as patch, minor, major, or security related.
Draft migration steps for breaking dependency and framework changes.
Create test, compatibility, rollback, and monitoring checklists.
Plan incremental upgrade batches based on risk and priority.
limitations: It does not guarantee that generated commands are safe to run unchanged.
It depends on accurate package metadata, changelogs, and test results.
It cannot replace maintainer review for major framework migrations.
It does not execute rollback validation unless the user approves commands.

use_cases.title: Upgrade a Web Application Safely
use_cases.description: Create a staged plan for npm package updates with tests, compatibility checks, and rollback steps.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Major Framework Migration
use_cases.description: Identify likely breaking changes and create a migration guide for React, Vue, Angular, or similar frameworks.

use_cases.title: Triage Security Updates
use_cases.description: Prioritize vulnerable dependencies and separate urgent security fixes from routine patch and minor updates.

prompt_templates.title: Review Available Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my dependency list and group updates by patch, minor, major, and security risk. Explain which updates should happen first.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Upgrade Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged upgrade plan for this project. Include pre-checks, test commands to review, rollback steps, and expected risks.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Migration Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a migration guide for upgrading this framework from the current version to the target version. Include breaking changes and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Upgrade Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this dependency upgrade pull request. Identify risky changes, missing tests, compatibility concerns, and rollback gaps before merge.

output_examples.input: A React project needs to move from React 17 to React 18.
output_examples.output: A migration plan with risk level, key breaking changes, staged update steps, test focus areas, and rollback checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A repository has many outdated npm dependencies and several security advisories.
output_examples.output: A priority matrix that separates urgent security fixes, low-risk patch updates, minor updates, and major migrations.

output_examples.input: A team wants to verify a dependency upgrade before release.
output_examples.output: A validation checklist covering unit tests, integration tests, build checks, compatibility review, and post-upgrade monitoring.

best_practices: Review every generated command before execution, especially package-manager and rollback commands.
Upgrade in small batches with a clean git state and passing tests before each step.
Read official migration guides and changelogs for major version changes.
anti_patterns: Running broad upgrades on a dirty working tree without a rollback point.
Applying codemods or npx commands from untrusted packages without review.
Merging dependency changes without focused tests for affected runtime paths.

faq.question: Does this skill run dependency upgrades automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides plans and command examples. Users should review and approve commands before execution.

faq.question: Which package ecosystems does it cover?
faq.answer: The examples focus on npm and Python packages, with framework guidance for Angular, React, and Vue.

faq.question: Can it detect breaking changes?
faq.answer: It can help identify likely breaking changes from changelogs, known patterns, and framework-specific upgrade notes.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with normal engineering controls: clean git state, command review, tests, backups, and staged rollout.

faq.question: Can it create rollback plans?
faq.answer: Yes. It can outline rollback procedures, but teams must adapt them to their repository and deployment system.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but its examples include commands that can modify files or execute package code.
